2000.assessment: Debt/GDP ~82%. Structural fiscal deficit. Government revenue ~10% of GDP. Civil servant salary arrears chronic. Large deficit with high debt. Budget effectively dependent on donor support. Large deficit, high debt.

2001.assessment: Debt/GDP ~88%. Coup attempt disrupted revenue collection. Civil service arrears. IMF program off-track. Large deficit, high debt.

2002.assessment: Debt/GDP ~95%. Bozizé insurgency — revenue collapse in conflict areas. Government near-insolvent. Unsustainable debt approaching crisis level.

2003.assessment: Debt/GDP ~103%. Bozizé coup. Government institutions dissolved — revenue collection halted. International donors suspended. Fiscal collapse during coup period.

sources.citation: IMF: CAR Article IV 2004 — coup fiscal impact

2004.assessment: Debt/GDP ~99%. Post-coup — some donor re-engagement. Revenue recovering slightly. IMF PRGF discussions. Still unsustainable debt level.

2005.assessment: Debt/GDP ~94%. Bozizé elections. IMF PRGF program started. Revenue collection improving. Still unsustainable but improving trajectory.

2006.assessment: Debt/GDP ~87%. HIPC decision point reached — debt relief process begun. Revenue improving with commodity prices. Large deficit but declining trajectory.

sources.citation: IMF/World Bank: CAR HIPC decision point 2006

2007.assessment: Debt/GDP ~65%. HIPC completion point 2009 anticipated — creditors providing relief. Significant debt reduction. Revenue improving. Large deficit remaining but debt declining dramatically. Moderate deficit, elevated debt.

2008.assessment: Debt/GDP ~58%. HIPC relief reducing debt burden significantly. Revenue improving with commodity prices. Manageable debt, small deficit.

2009.assessment: Debt/GDP ~45%. HIPC completion point reached 2009 — major multilateral debt cancellation. Best fiscal position in decades. Revenue adequate. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2010.assessment: Debt/GDP ~43%. Post-HIPC. Revenue collection improving. Some civil service arrears resolved. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2011.assessment: Debt/GDP ~45%. Bozizé elections (disputed). Post-HIPC trajectory continuing. Slight deficit. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2012.assessment: Debt/GDP ~47%. Séléka forming December — confidence weakening. Revenue still flowing. Small deficit manageable but deteriorating.

2013.assessment: Debt/GDP ~53% but fiscal situation catastrophic. Séléka coup destroyed revenue system. Tax collection collapsed. International donors suspended. Customs revenue nil. Government employees unpaid for months. Fiscal collapse in practice despite moderate debt/GDP ratio.

2014.assessment: Debt/GDP ~58%. Civil war. Revenue minimal. International emergency support providing some fiscal life support. Transitional government with no real budget. Fiscal collapse.

2015.assessment: Debt/GDP ~60%. Bangui Forum. Some revenue restoration. Transitional government with modest fiscal capacity. Moderate deficit, elevated debt.

2016.assessment: Debt/GDP ~59%. Touadéra elected — international legitimacy restored. Donor support increased. Revenue improving. Moderate deficit, elevated but manageable debt.

2017.assessment: Debt/GDP ~61%. Worst violence since 2014 — some revenue disruption. International support continued. Moderate deficit, elevated debt.

2018.assessment: Debt/GDP ~60%. Wagner entering. Some security/economic improvement. Khartoum process. IMF program. Moderate deficit, elevated debt.

2019.assessment: Debt/GDP ~55%. APRC agreement. Continuing debt reduction. Revenue improving. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2020.assessment: Debt/GDP ~62%. CPC offensive December 2020. COVID fiscal support needed. Debt rising again. Moderate deficit, elevated debt.

2021.assessment: Debt/GDP ~59%. Wagner defeated CPC — security improvement benefited revenue. Debt declining. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2022.assessment: Debt/GDP ~54%. Africa Corps. Constitutional referendum. Revenue at post-2013 recovery high. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2023.assessment: Debt/GDP ~52%. Africa Corps consolidating. Constitutional referendum extended term limits. Fiscal improvement continuing. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2024.assessment: Debt/GDP ~51% estimated. Africa Corps. 2025 elections approaching. Fiscal improving but volatile. Small deficit.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Debt/GDP ~50% projected. Elections. Africa Corps. Small deficit, manageable debt.

2000.assessment: CAR one of world's poorest. GDP per capita ~$262. Political instability under Patassé, coup attempts. Economy heavily dependent on subsistence agriculture, diamonds, timber. Near peak relative to historical baseline. Moderate decline from 1980s levels.

2001.assessment: GDP ~$261, essentially flat from 2000. May 2001 coup attempt disrupted economic activity. Libyan forces involvement. Continued political instability deterred investment. Moderate decline.

2002.assessment: GDP ~$251, slight decline. Bozizé insurgency from Chad. Conflict in northern provinces disrupted trade and agriculture. Significant decline from better years.

2003.assessment: GDP ~$263. Bozizé coup March 2003 — economic disruption but Bangui restored some stability post-coup. Transitional period. Moderate decline from potential.

2004.assessment: GDP ~$294, some recovery. Bozizé government received some donor support. Transitional process. Commodity prices improving. Moderate decline from peer baselines.

2005.assessment: GDP ~$344. Bozizé elected, international legitimacy restored. IMF program. Some recovery. Still very low — significant decline from peers and potential.

2006.assessment: GDP ~$370. UFDR captured Birao — northern conflict. But Bangui economy recovering. Commodity prices (diamonds, cotton, timber) improving. Significant decline but recovering trajectory.

2007.assessment: GDP ~$431. Birao peace deal. Some rebel integration. International commodity boom benefiting CAR exports. Bush War continuing but economy growing. Significant decline from peers but improving.

2008.assessment: GDP ~$453. Libreville peace process. Global financial crisis limited impact on subsistence economy. Commodity prices peaked then fell H2. Near peak of 2000s growth.

2009.assessment: GDP ~$446. Global financial crisis impact — commodity demand fell. CPA implementation. LRA raids increasing. Slight GDP dip. Significant decline from potential.

2010.assessment: GDP ~$466. Post-crisis recovery. Commodity prices recovering. Relative peace in Bangui. Bozizé government stable period. Significant decline from peer averages.

2011.assessment: GDP ~$494. Near decade peak. Bozizé elections (disputed). Some diamond and timber exports. Significant decline from potential but improving trajectory before Séléka formation.

2012.assessment: GDP ~$505, approximate peak for this era. Séléka forming in December — anticipating economic disruption. Economy still functioning in most of country. Significant decline from global peers.

2013.assessment: GDP ~$350 — ~30% collapse from 2012 peak. Séléka coup March 2013. Commerce destroyed in Bangui. Farming disrupted throughout country. Diamond/timber exports halted. International sanctions. Severe decline from peak.

2014.assessment: GDP ~$362. Civil war continuing — anti-Balaka vs ex-Séléka. MINUSCA deployed December. Samba-Panza transitional. Minimal economic recovery from 2013 collapse. Severe decline.

2015.assessment: GDP ~$359, minimal change. Bangui Forum. New constitution. Still at severe decline level — armed groups controlling most territory preventing economic activity. Severe decline.

2016.assessment: GDP ~$382. Touadéra elected, some international confidence restored. Slow recovery from 2013 collapse. Armed groups still dominating hinterland. Severe decline — still 25% below pre-coup peak.

2017.assessment: GDP ~$403. Worst violence since 2014 in some areas. Recovery continuing despite conflict. International aid supporting Bangui economy. Severe decline — below 2012 peak.

2018.assessment: GDP ~$416. Wagner Group entering late 2017-2018. Khartoum process. Some security improvement with Russian involvement. Slow recovery. Severe decline — still 18% below 2012 peak.

2019.assessment: GDP ~$428. APRC peace agreement February 2019. Some territory reclaimed with Wagner/FACA. Slow recovery continuing. Still severely below 2012 peak. Significant decline.

2020.assessment: GDP ~$420. CPC offensive December 2020. COVID impact minimal (subsistence economy). CPC offensive disrupted recovery. Significant decline.

2021.assessment: GDP ~$468. Wagner defeated CPC. Territory recovery. International commodity prices rising. Significant recovery from CPC disruption. Approaching 2012 peak levels. Significant decline from potential.

2022.assessment: GDP ~$501. Near 2012 levels again. Africa Corps consolidating. Some trade routes reopened. Timber/diamond exports recovering. Moderate decline from long-term potential.

2023.assessment: GDP ~$521. Exceeds 2012 peak in nominal terms but inflation-adjusted may be similar. Constitutional referendum extended term limits. Moderate decline from potential.

2024.assessment: GDP ~$530 estimated. Slow growth continuing. Africa Corps presence stabilizing some areas. Moderate decline from development potential.

2025.assessment: March 2025: GDP ~$540 projected. Slow growth. 2025 elections approaching. Africa Corps presence. Moderate decline from development potential.

2000.assessment: Inflation ~3.2%. CFA franc peg provided anchor. Political instability under Patassé but CFA monetary union limited currency-driven inflation. Low to moderate.

2001.assessment: Inflation ~3.7%. Coup attempt May 2001 caused brief price spikes in Bangui. CFA franc peg held. Moderate.

2002.assessment: Inflation ~2.2%. Below 3%. Bozizé insurgency in north limited impact on overall prices. CFA maintained. Low.

2003.assessment: Inflation ~4.4%. Bozizé coup disruption caused supply chain disruption and price increases in Bangui. CFA peg prevented runaway inflation. Moderate.

2004.assessment: Deflation ~-2.1%. Post-coup stabilization — food prices fell. International food aid suppressing prices. Unusual deflation year.

2005.assessment: Inflation ~2.9%. Near threshold. Bozizé election, IMF program. CFA peg. Low.

2006.assessment: Inflation ~6.7%. UFDR conflict in north disrupted supply chains — higher food prices. Moderate inflation.

2007.assessment: Inflation ~0.9%. Very low. Birao peace deal. CFA franc strong. Low.

2008.assessment: Inflation ~9.3%. Global food price crisis 2007-2008 hit CAR hard — food import prices spiked. Near elevated threshold but still moderate.

2009.assessment: Inflation ~3.5%. Post-food-crisis normalization. Global commodity prices fell. CFA peg stable. Moderate.

2010.assessment: Inflation ~1.4%. Very low. Stable period. CFA peg strong. Low.

2011.assessment: Inflation ~1.2%. Very low. Bozizé elections. CFA peg maintained. Low.

2012.assessment: Inflation ~5.9%. Séléka coalition forming December 2012 — fear premium in markets. Supply chain disruption H2. Moderate.

2013.assessment: Official annual CPI ~6.6%, but actual experience was worse. Séléka coup March 2013 caused severe price spikes in Bangui — looting destroyed commercial infrastructure. CFA peg technically maintained but informal markets saw much higher prices. Annual average masks coup-period severity. Elevated in practice.

2014.assessment: Inflation ~11.6%. Civil war disrupted food supply and trade routes. Humanitarian crisis. MINUSCA arrival. Elevated inflation during active conflict.

2015.assessment: Inflation ~4.5%. Declining from 2014 peak. Bangui Forum, new constitution. Humanitarian aid suppressing prices in Bangui. Moderate.

2016.assessment: Inflation ~4.6%. Touadéra elected. Gradual normalization. Moderate.

2017.assessment: Inflation ~4.1%. Rural violence ongoing but Bangui prices moderated. International aid economy. Moderate.

2018.assessment: Inflation ~1.6%. Wagner entering. Khartoum process. International donors. CFA peg. Low.

2019.assessment: Inflation ~2.8%. APRC agreement. Relative stability. CFA franc peg. Low.

2020.assessment: Inflation ~2.7%. CPC offensive December 2020 causing supply disruptions. COVID minimal on subsistence economy. Low.

2021.assessment: Inflation ~4.3%. Post-CPC normalization. Global commodity inflation 2021. Wagner defeating CPC. Moderate.

2022.assessment: Inflation ~5.6%. Global energy and food inflation from Russia-Ukraine war affecting import prices. CFA peg providing anchor. Moderate.

2023.assessment: Inflation ~3.1%. Post-global inflation normalization. Constitutional referendum. Africa Corps. Moderate.

2024.assessment: Inflation ~3.4% estimated. Africa Corps era. Relative stability. Moderate.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Inflation ~3.5% projected. Elections approaching. Moderate.

2000.assessment: Trade/GDP ~38%. Landlocked economy dependent on Cameroon route. Cotton, diamonds, timber exports. Moderate trade openness for sub-Saharan Africa. Political instability reducing trade somewhat. Restricted but not closed.

2001.assessment: Trade/GDP ~37%. Coup attempt reduced commercial activity. Small decline. Restricted.

2002.assessment: Trade/GDP ~35%. Bozizé insurgency disrupted northern trade routes. Some export collapse. Restricted.

2003.assessment: Trade/GDP ~42%. Bozizé coup disrupted production but imports may have spiked with coup-related flows. Moderate trade openness despite political disruption.

2004.assessment: Trade/GDP ~44%. Post-coup stabilization; international donor imports boosting trade figure. Some export recovery. Moderate.

2005.assessment: Trade/GDP ~42%. Bozizé elected, IMF program — some trade normalization. Moderate.

2006.assessment: Trade/GDP ~39%. UFDR captured Birao — northern trade disrupted. Overall restricted.

2007.assessment: Trade/GDP ~43%. Birao peace deal. Commodity boom benefiting exports. Moderate openness.

2008.assessment: Trade/GDP ~47%. Global commodity boom peak. Timber, cotton, diamonds exports high. Moderate openness approaching pre-conflict levels.

2009.assessment: Trade/GDP ~41%. Global crisis reduced commodity demand. Moderate openness — declining from 2008 peak.

2010.assessment: Trade/GDP ~45%. Post-crisis recovery. Commodity prices recovering. Moderate openness.

2011.assessment: Trade/GDP ~47%. Pre-Séléka period — highest trade openness of decade. Bozizé elections. Moderate.

2012.assessment: Trade/GDP ~43%. Séléka forming — declining commercial confidence. Moderate but declining.

2013.assessment: Trade/GDP ~27%. Severe collapse from Séléka coup. Exports (diamonds, timber, cotton) halted. Trade routes disrupted. International sanctions. Restricted trade — collapse from moderate levels.

2014.assessment: Trade/GDP ~26%. Civil war. Trade routes insecure. Diamond/timber exports minimal. International aid imports somewhat. Heavily restricted.

2015.assessment: Trade/GDP ~28%. Slight recovery. Bangui Forum. Some commercial normalization in Bangui. Restricted.

2016.assessment: Trade/GDP ~31%. Touadéra elected — some international confidence. Diamond exports partially resuming. Restricted but recovering.

2017.assessment: Trade/GDP ~30%. Worst violence since 2014 — trade routes insecure. Restricted.

2018.assessment: Trade/GDP ~33%. Wagner entering — some security improvement on routes. Khartoum process. Restricted but recovering.

2019.assessment: Trade/GDP ~37%. APRC agreement. Wagner/FACA territory recovery. Some export routes reopened. Restricted but improving.

2020.assessment: Trade/GDP ~34%. CPC offensive December 2020. COVID reduced global demand. Restricted.

2021.assessment: Trade/GDP ~39%. Wagner defeated CPC. Trade routes recovering. Global commodity rebound. Near restricted/moderate boundary.

2022.assessment: Trade/GDP ~43%. Africa Corps consolidating. Trade routes somewhat more secure. Returning near pre-2013 coup levels. Moderate openness.

2023.assessment: Trade/GDP ~44%. Africa Corps. Constitutional referendum. Moderate openness recovering to pre-coup levels.

2024.assessment: Trade/GDP ~44% estimated. Africa Corps. Stable routes. Moderate.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Trade/GDP ~45% projected. Elections approaching. Moderate openness.

2000.assessment: Formal unemployment effectively unmeasured. Economy ~80% subsistence agriculture. Small civil service and commercial sector. Political instability under Patassé. Very high effective unemployment in formal sense; subsistence absorbs most. Very high informal underemployment.

2001.assessment: Coup attempt May 2001. Formal sector disrupted. Government civil service partially non-functional. Very high effective unemployment — subsistence agriculture dominant.

2002.assessment: Bozizé insurgency. Northern provinces destabilized — agricultural activity disrupted. Formal sector minimal. Very high effective unemployment.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. Government institutions dissolved. Civil service collapsed temporarily. Formal sector disrupted. Very high effective unemployment during coup period.

2004.assessment: Transitional period. Formal sector partially recovering. Bozizé government restoring some civil service. International donors returning. Very high effective unemployment.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected. IMF program. Civil service beginning to receive salaries more regularly. Some formal sector recovery. Very high effective unemployment but formal sector marginally improving.

2006.assessment: UFDR captured Birao. Northern conflict. Bush War continuing — millions displaced internally. Very high.

2007.assessment: Birao peace deal. Some rebel integration. Formal sector minimal. Bangui economy slightly improving. Very high overall.

2008.assessment: Libreville peace process. International commodity boom. Mining/logging sector slight expansion. Very high structural unemployment but formal sector marginally active.

2009.assessment: LRA invasion from Uganda. Population displacement in east. Formal sector minimal. Very high effective unemployment.

2010.assessment: Relative stability in Bangui. Formal sector tiny but functioning. Very high structural unemployment — subsistence dominant.

2011.assessment: Bozizé elections (disputed). Formal sector in Bangui functioning. Pre-Séléka period. Very high structural unemployment.

2012.assessment: Séléka forming December 2012 — business confidence collapsing. Anticipatory economic contraction. Very high.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Formal sector collapsed. Commerce destroyed in Bangui looting. Government employees not paid. Entire formal economy obliterated. Crisis-level unemployment in formal sector. Subsistence essential for survival.

2014.assessment: Civil war. Formal sector near-zero. Over 1 million IDPs. MINUSCA economy replacing formal sector for some. Complete collapse of formal employment. Crisis.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum. Some government functions restored. International aid economy supporting some employment. Formal sector minimal. Crisis-level in formal terms but subsistence absorbing most.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elected. Some formal sector recovery in Bangui. Armed groups controlling hinterland. Very high formal unemployment across country.

2017.assessment: Worst violence since 2014. New displacement. Formal sector confined to Bangui. Crisis-level effective unemployment.

2018.assessment: Wagner entering. Some security improvement. Khartoum process. Formal employment still very limited. Very high.

2019.assessment: APRC agreement. Wagner/FACA expanding territory. Some return migration. Formal sector slight recovery. Very high.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive December 2020 — new displacement, trade disruption. COVID minimal on subsistence. Very high.

2021.assessment: Wagner defeated CPC. Territory recovering. Some trade routes reopened. Formal employment marginally improving from 2020 CPC disruption. Very high.

2022.assessment: Africa Corps consolidating. Some economic recovery. Constitutional referendum. Formal sector tiny but functioning in Bangui. Very high structural unemployment.

2023.assessment: Wagner → Africa Corps transition. Some economic normalization. Very high structural unemployment continuing.

2024.assessment: Africa Corps presence. 2025 elections approaching. Formal sector in Bangui. Very high structural unemployment.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Very high structural unemployment continues. Subsistence agriculture dominant. Formal sector minimal.

2000.assessment: Patassé government recognized internationally. AU/UN member. Embassies in Bangui from major powers (France, US). International engagement normal. Normal minor disputes.

sources.citation: UN General Assembly: CAR member state

2001.assessment: Coup attempt raised international concern but did not affect formal relations. AU engaged. International community supported dialogue. Normal minor disputes.

2002.assessment: Bozizé insurgency from Chad. International community concerned. Patassé government recognized. Normal diplomatic relations but instability raising concerns. Normal minor disputes.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. AU suspended CAR from activities temporarily. Western governments suspended aid briefly. France continued engagement (had forces in Bangui). Brief partial isolation then rapid restoration. Partial isolation.

2004.assessment: AU suspension lifted. Transitional process beginning. International community re-engaging. Diplomatic normalization. Normal minor disputes.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected — full international legitimacy restored. IMF program commenced. France maintained forces. Normal full international relations.

2006.assessment: France Opération Boali supporting Bozizé government against UFDR. International community engaged. ECCAS MICOPAX. Full relations active.

2007.assessment: Birao peace deal. International community supporting peace process. MICOPAX. Full relations active.

2008.assessment: Libreville peace process. International mediation. Full engagement. Full relations active.

2009.assessment: HIPC completion — IMF/World Bank full engagement. International community supporting Bozizé. Full relations.

2010.assessment: Normal international relations. Full diplomatic integration. Full relations.

2011.assessment: Bozizé re-elected (disputed but accepted internationally). Full relations. MICOPAX continuing.

2012.assessment: Séléka forming — international community alarmed. Diplomatic activity increasing. Bozizé government seeking support. Normal minor disputes — formal relations normal but crisis looming.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. AU suspended CAR from activities. Western donors suspended aid. ECOWAS condemned. But Djotodia installed — some international engagement for humanitarian purposes. Significant isolation — Séléka government internationally isolated, but transition to Samba-Panza in January 2014 began restoration.

2014.assessment: Samba-Panza transitional (January 2014). AU restored engagement. France Opération Sangaris December 2013. MISCA (AU) deployed. MINUSCA (UN) September 2014. International community extensively re-engaged. Partial isolation giving way to full international humanitarian engagement.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum July 2015. International community fully engaged in peace process. MINUSCA. EUFOR RCA 2014-2015. Full international engagement for transitional process. Normal minor disputes.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elected — full legitimacy. France ended Opération Sangaris October 2016. MINUSCA continuing. Full international relations.

2017.assessment: Full international relations. MINUSCA. Rome peace talks (Sant'Egidio mediation). Violence ongoing but international engagement maintained. Full relations active.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group entering — Western concerns about Russian military presence. Khartoum process (Sudan/Russia mediation). MINUSCA continuing. Western relations strained by Wagner but formal diplomatic relations maintained. Normal minor disputes — with growing Wagner-related tensions.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: CAR Wagner 2018

2019.assessment: APRC agreement — international community broadly supported. MINUSCA. Wagner deepening — Western criticism increasing. Normal minor disputes with growing Russia/West tension over CAR.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive. MINUSCA. Russia/Wagner openly conducting combat operations. Some Western governments reduced engagement with Touadéra government over Wagner. Partial isolation from Western donors. Partial isolation.

2021.assessment: Wagner defeated CPC. Touadéra re-elected. Western governments (US, EU, France) increasingly critical of Wagner and CAR government association. UN human rights reports on Wagner atrocities. Partial isolation from West, deep engagement with Russia.

sources.citation: UN Human Rights: CAR Wagner atrocities 2021

2022.assessment: Russia-Ukraine war: CAR sided with Russia in UN votes. France expelled from CAR. EU/US critical of constitutional referendum extending term limits. Western diplomatic missions reduced. Partial isolation from Western bloc.

2023.assessment: Wagner → Africa Corps June 2023. Constitutional referendum extended term limits — international criticism. Western isolation deepening. Russia/China maintaining full relations. MINUSCA mandate under pressure from CAR government. Partial isolation from West.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2023 — Africa Corps, Western relations

2024.assessment: Africa Corps entrenched. Western relations strained. MINUSCA reduced in scope. Russia/China partnership strong. Elections approaching. Partial isolation from Western bloc.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Africa Corps. Western relations strained. Elections approaching. Russia/China partnership. Partial isolation from Western bloc continuing.

2000.assessment: FDI ~0.3% of GDP. Minimal. Political instability under Patassé. Landlocked, poor infrastructure, governance issues deterring investment. Minimal FDI.

2001.assessment: FDI ~0.2% of GDP. Very minimal. Coup attempt further deterring investment. Minimal.

2002.assessment: FDI ~0.2% of GDP. Bozizé insurgency. Investment minimal. Minimal.

2003.assessment: FDI ~0.1% of GDP. Bozizé coup. Investment near zero during political transition. Minimal.

2004.assessment: FDI ~0.4% of GDP. Some return of investor confidence post-coup. Timber sector investment. Minimal.

2005.assessment: FDI ~0.5% of GDP. Bozizé elected — some investor confidence. Timber/diamond exploration. Very low.

2006.assessment: FDI ~0.8% of GDP. Some commodity sector interest. UFDR conflict limiting investment outside Bangui. Very low.

2007.assessment: FDI ~1.1% of GDP. Global commodity boom. Some mining/logging investment. Birao peace deal. Low FDI.

2008.assessment: FDI ~1.5% of GDP. Global commodity peak. Timber and diamond sector investment. Libreville peace. Low FDI — slightly improving.

2009.assessment: FDI ~0.8% of GDP. Global financial crisis reduced commodity investment. HIPC completion — some positive signal. Very low.

2010.assessment: FDI ~1.0% of GDP. Post-crisis recovery. Mining sector re-engaging. Low.

2011.assessment: FDI ~1.2% of GDP. Bozizé re-elected. Commodity boom continuing. Mining/logging peak. Low.

2012.assessment: FDI ~0.9% of GDP. Séléka forming — investor confidence collapsing. Very low due to crisis anticipation.

2013.assessment: FDI ~0.1% of GDP. Séléka coup March 2013. All investment fled. Kimberley Process suspended. International sanctions. Virtually no FDI. Minimal.

2014.assessment: FDI ~0.1% of GDP. Civil war. No commercial investment. International aid only. Minimal.

2015.assessment: FDI ~0.2% of GDP. Bangui Forum. Some commercial tentative recovery. KP partial restoration. Minimal.

2016.assessment: FDI ~0.4% of GDP. Touadéra elected — some investor confidence. Timber and diamond sectors beginning to resume. Minimal.

2017.assessment: FDI ~0.5% of GDP. Violence continuing but some mining investment. Russian interests beginning. Very low.

2018.assessment: FDI ~0.8% of GDP. Wagner entering — Russian mining investment (gold, diamonds). Lobaye Invest (Wagner-linked) operating. This largely opaque extraction. Very low formal FDI; actual Russian investment higher but not reported.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner mining CAR 2018

2019.assessment: FDI ~1.0% of GDP. APRC. Wagner/Russian mining expanding. Some Chinese interest. Low FDI (standard); Russian opaque extraction significant.

2020.assessment: FDI ~0.6% of GDP. CPC offensive. Global COVID. Investment retreating. Very low.

2021.assessment: FDI ~0.9% of GDP. Wagner defeated CPC. Commodity recovery. Russian mining investment dominant. Very low formal FDI.

2022.assessment: FDI ~1.1% of GDP. Africa Corps era. Russian/Chinese mining investment. Constitutional referendum. Low formal FDI with significant opaque Russian extraction.

2023.assessment: FDI ~1.0% of GDP. Africa Corps. Wagner → Africa Corps transition. Mining investment continuing. Low.

2024.assessment: FDI ~1.0% of GDP estimated. Africa Corps. Elections approaching. Low.

2025.assessment: March 2025: FDI ~1.0% projected. Africa Corps. Elections. Low investment environment.

2000.assessment: France Opération Boali providing support to government. Small French contingent in Bangui providing training and rapid reaction. Limited invited advisors.

2001.assessment: France Opération Boali. Coup attempt — French forces provided backup. Limited invited advisors.

2002.assessment: France Opération Boali. FOMUC (CEMAC peacekeeping) deployed late 2002 with ~380 troops after Bozizé insurgency. Significant invited forces.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup — French forces in Bangui did not intervene but facilitated. Chad forces supporting Bozizé (uninvited by Patassé government). FOMUC continued. Contested/mixed presence — Chad uninvited by previous government, France ambiguous.

2004.assessment: France Boali continuing. FOMUC. Bozizé government inviting both. Significant invited forces.

2005.assessment: France Boali. FOMUC. Bozizé elected government. Significant invited forces.

2006.assessment: France Opération Boali — expanded operations against UFDR in Birao. Retook Birao with French air and ground support. FOMUC. Significant invited forces conducting active combat.

2007.assessment: France Boali. FOMUC transitioning to MICOPAX (ECCAS, 2008). Peace process. Significant invited forces.

2008.assessment: France Boali. MICOPAX (ECCAS, ~500 troops) replacing FOMUC. Libreville peace. Significant invited forces.

2009.assessment: France Boali. MICOPAX. Relative stability. Significant invited forces.

2010.assessment: France Boali (limited). MICOPAX. Significant invited forces.

2011.assessment: France Boali. MICOPAX. US AFRICOM forces briefly deployed for LRA operations in east. Significant invited forces.

2012.assessment: France Boali. MICOPAX. Séléka forming — Bozizé government requesting international military support. Significant invited forces.

2013.assessment: France Opération Sangaris deployed December 2013 (~1,600 troops) in response to Séléka coup and humanitarian crisis. MISCA (AU, 6,000 troops) deployed December 2013. MICOPAX ended. France conducting active combat operations. Active foreign combat operations by invited forces.

2014.assessment: France Sangaris. MISCA → MINUSCA September 2014 (~12,000 troops). EUFOR RCA deployed April 2014. Multiple foreign forces conducting active combat/peacekeeping. Active foreign combat operations.

2015.assessment: France Sangaris (~900 troops). MINUSCA (~11,000 troops). EUFOR RCA ended March 2015. Sangaris winding down. Active foreign combat operations.

2016.assessment: France Sangaris ended October 2016. MINUSCA (~12,500 troops) continuing. Touadéra elected. Significant invited forces (MINUSCA).

2017.assessment: MINUSCA (~12,500 troops). Russian military instructors beginning to arrive (formal Bilateral Military Agreement November 2017). Significant invited forces with Russian advisory expansion starting.

2018.assessment: MINUSCA. Wagner Group operational (~300-700 personnel) — conducting combat, mining operations, government training. Khartoum peace process. Multiple foreign forces: UN peacekeepers + Wagner paramilitaries. Active foreign combat operations — Wagner conducting offensive operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner in CAR 2018

2019.assessment: MINUSCA. Wagner (~1,000 personnel) — expanding operations. APRC agreement. Wagner conducting combat against armed groups, establishing parallel government structures. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner CAR 2019

2020.assessment: MINUSCA. Wagner expanded to ~1,500-2,000 personnel for CPC offensive response. Rwanda deployed 300 troops under bilateral agreement. CPC offensive triggered massive Wagner combat operations. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner CPC December 2020

2021.assessment: MINUSCA. Wagner (~2,000) defeated CPC with full combat operations. Rwanda troops. Sudan troops beginning. Multiple foreign forces conducting active combat. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner CAR 2021
UN Human Rights: Wagner atrocities CAR 2021

2022.assessment: MINUSCA (reduced scope). Wagner (~1,500-2,000) consolidating control. France expelled — French forces gone. Rwanda, Sudan forces. Wagner conducting combat and governance. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner CAR 2022

2023.assessment: Wagner → Africa Corps June 2023 (seamless transition, same personnel ~1,500). MINUSCA (strained relationship). Africa Corps conducting combat operations, training FACA, extractive operations. Active foreign combat operations.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Africa Corps CAR 2023

2024.assessment: Africa Corps entrenched. MINUSCA. Elections approaching. Africa Corps conducting combat and governance functions. Active foreign combat operations.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Africa Corps continuing operations. MINUSCA. Elections. Active foreign combat operations continuing.

2000.assessment: Minimal displacement. Patassé era political instability but no major displacement events. Some returns from earlier conflicts. Stable minimal.

2001.assessment: Coup attempt May 2001 caused some displacement in Bangui. ~15,000-20,000 new IDPs. Less than 1% of population. Moderate outflows.

2002.assessment: Bozizé insurgency from Chad — significant displacement in northern provinces. ~50,000-60,000 displaced from conflict zone. About 1.5% of population. Significant outflows.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. Fighting in Bangui and across country. ~90,000-100,000 displaced (internal and refugees to DRC/Chad). ~2.5% of population. Significant outflows.

2004.assessment: Post-coup stabilization. Some returns to Bangui. Continued displacement in north from rebel groups. ~40,000 remaining displaced. ~1% of population. Moderate outflows.

2005.assessment: Bush War escalating. Northern rebel groups (UFDR, FPR) active. ~60,000 IDPs in northern provinces. Some cross-border to Chad. Significant outflows.

2006.assessment: UFDR captured Birao November 2006. Major displacement from Vakaga and Ouham prefectures. ~140,000 IDPs. ~3.5% of population (~4M). Mass displacement.

2007.assessment: Peak Bush War displacement. ~200,000 IDPs. UFDR conflict, FDPC, APRD all active. Some cross-border to Chad and Sudan. ~4% of ~4M population. Mass displacement.

2008.assessment: Libreville peace process — some displacement decline. ~150,000 IDPs. Bush War gradually calming with peace agreements. Mass displacement declining.

2009.assessment: Peace agreements reducing displacement. LRA invasion from Uganda causing new displacement in east. ~100,000 IDPs. Significant outflows but declining.

2010.assessment: Declining displacement. Peace agreements holding nominally. ~70,000 IDPs remaining. LRA still in east. Significant outflows.

2011.assessment: ~60,000 IDPs. Bozizé re-elected. Pre-Séléka relative stability improving displacement numbers. Significant outflows.

2012.assessment: Séléka forming December 2012 — rapid displacement escalation in H2. ~100,000 displaced. Significant outflows rising.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Massive displacement. By end 2013: ~600,000 IDPs + ~270,000 refugees in DRC, Chad, Cameroon. Total ~870,000 displaced out of ~4.3M population (~20%). Catastrophic displacement.

2014.assessment: Peak displacement year. ~700,000 IDPs + ~420,000 refugees = ~1.12M displaced. Population ~4.5M. ~25% displaced. Civil war Séléka vs anti-Balaka. MINUSCA deployed. Catastrophic displacement.

2015.assessment: ~520,000 IDPs + ~500,000 refugees = ~1.02M displaced out of ~4.7M population (~22%). Bangui Forum nominal ceasefire. Catastrophic displacement.

2016.assessment: ~400,000 IDPs + ~500,000 refugees = ~900,000 displaced. Touadéra elected. Population ~4.8M — ~18%. Still catastrophic despite Touadéra election optimism.

2017.assessment: Worst violence since 2014 — displacement increased again. ~600,000 IDPs + ~520,000 refugees = ~1.12M displaced. Population ~5.0M — ~22%. Catastrophic.

2018.assessment: ~600,000 IDPs + ~560,000 refugees = ~1.16M displaced. Wagner entering. Khartoum process. Population ~5.1M — ~23%. Catastrophic.

2019.assessment: APRC February 2019. Some IDPs returning. ~600,000 IDPs + ~590,000 refugees = ~1.19M. Population ~5.2M — ~23%. Some return of IDPs but refugee numbers still high. Catastrophic.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive December 2020 — new mass displacement. ~650,000 IDPs + ~600,000 refugees = ~1.25M. Population ~5.3M — ~24%. Catastrophic.

2021.assessment: Wagner defeated CPC — some IDP return. ~550,000 IDPs + ~600,000 refugees = ~1.15M. Population ~5.4M — ~21%. Still catastrophic despite CPC defeat.

2022.assessment: ~450,000 IDPs + ~700,000 refugees = ~1.15M displaced. Population ~5.4M — ~21%. Some IDP improvement but refugee stocks growing. Catastrophic.

2023.assessment: Africa Corps. ~400,000 IDPs + ~730,000 refugees = ~1.13M. Population ~5.5M — ~21%. Gradual IDP reduction with security consolidation. Catastrophic.

2024.assessment: ~350,000 IDPs + ~730,000 refugees = ~1.08M. Population ~5.6M — ~19%. Gradual improvement continuing. Elections approaching. Catastrophic.

2025.assessment: March 2025: ~300,000-350,000 IDPs + ~700,000+ refugees = ~1M displaced. Population ~5.6M — ~18%. Gradual improvement but still catastrophic overall displacement burden. Catastrophic.

2000.assessment: No international sanctions on CAR. Normal international relations. Patassé government recognized. AU/UN member in good standing. No sanctions.

sources.citation: UN Security Council: CAR — no sanctions regime pre-2013

2001.assessment: No international sanctions. Coup attempt May 2001 did not trigger sanctions. International community called for restraint. No sanctions.

2002.assessment: No international sanctions. Bozizé insurgency — international community concerned but no sanctions applied. No sanctions.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. AU briefly suspended CAR membership, then restored after transitional government process. No formal sanctions applied. Brief suspension then restoration. Targeted individuals only in practice (not formal sanctions).

2004.assessment: AU suspension lifted. Bozizé government pursuing legitimacy. No formal sanctions. Normal international relations restored. No sanctions.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected. International legitimacy restored. IMF program. No sanctions. Full international relations.

2006.assessment: No sanctions. International community supporting Bozizé government. French Opération Boali providing security. No sanctions.

2007.assessment: No sanctions. Birao peace deal. MICOPAX (ECCAS peacekeeping) present. International support for peace process. No sanctions.

2008.assessment: No sanctions. Libreville peace process. International engagement. No sanctions.

2009.assessment: No sanctions. HIPC completion — international donors supportive. No sanctions.

2010.assessment: No sanctions. Normal international relations. No sanctions.

2011.assessment: No sanctions. Bozizé re-elected (disputed). International community engaging. No sanctions.

2012.assessment: No sanctions yet despite Séléka coalition forming. International community concerned. UNSC monitoring. No formal sanctions — Séléka attacking government did not immediately trigger sanctions.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. AU suspended CAR membership. Kimberley Process suspended CAR diamond exports May 2013. UNSC Resolution 2127 December 2013 — arms embargo, targeted sanctions on armed group leaders. Sectoral (arms + diamonds) by year end.

2014.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. UNSC 2134 (January 2014) expanded sanctions — targeted individuals (armed group leaders). Samba-Panza transitional — international community restored engagement. Kimberley Process partial restoration process beginning. Targeted individuals only in practice (arms embargo + named individuals).

2015.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Targeted individual sanctions. Kimberley Process partially restored 2015 for government-controlled areas. Bangui Forum. New constitution. Targeted sanctions only.

2016.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Touadéra elected — international legitimacy restored. Targeted individual sanctions on armed group leaders. Operations Sangaris ended. Targeted individuals only.

2017.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Targeted individual sanctions on armed group leaders. Wagner entering — Western concerns about Russia bypassing arms embargo. Targeted individuals.

2018.assessment: Arms embargo continuing with Russia receiving UN exemption for military training. Wagner Group operating with claimed exemption. Targeted individual sanctions on armed group leaders. EU partially lifted restrictions to support Touadéra. Targeted individuals.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner arms embargo issues 2018

2019.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. APRC agreement — some armed group leaders sanctioned individually. EU/US targeted individuals. Targeted individuals only.

2020.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. CPC offensive December 2020. US and EU imposed additional targeted sanctions on CPC armed group leaders. Targeted individuals.

2021.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Wagner Group sanctioned by US and EU for human rights violations in CAR. UNSC arms embargo renewed. Targeted individuals — Wagner leaders added.

2022.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Russia invades Ukraine — Wagner expanded sanctions. CAR government criticized for Wagner association. Targeted individual sanctions on Wagner and armed group leaders. Constitutional referendum. Targeted individuals.

2023.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Wagner → Africa Corps June 2023. Africa Corps sanctioned by some Western governments. UNSC arms embargo renewed. Targeted individuals on armed group and Africa Corps leaders.

2024.assessment: Arms embargo continuing. Africa Corps presence. Targeted individual sanctions on armed group leaders. Targeted individuals.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Arms embargo and targeted individual sanctions continuing. Africa Corps. Elections approaching. Targeted individuals.

2000.assessment: Patassé era. Political opponents harassed. Press freedom limited. Opposition parties existing but suppressed. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2001.assessment: Coup attempt worsened civil liberties — arbitrary arrests, political detentions. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2002.assessment: War in north. Civil liberties suppressed in conflict zones. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup. Constitution suspended, political parties banned initially. FH downgraded to CL=7. No civil liberties during coup/transition.

2004.assessment: Transition restoring some formal civil liberties. FH improved slightly to CL=6. Political parties re-legalized. Severe restrictions.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elections — some electoral freedoms but constrained. Press still controlled. Opposition suppressed. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2006.assessment: Ongoing conflict. Civil liberties poor. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2007.assessment: Peace process. Marginal improvement noted. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2008.assessment: LRA attacks in southeast violated civil liberties. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2009.assessment: Some political space with dialogue. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2010.assessment: Election preparation. Civil liberties constrained. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2011.assessment: Bozizé re-elected in disputed elections. Opposition suppressed. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2012.assessment: Séléka advancing. Media freedom declining. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Atrocities, killings, looting. No civil liberties. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2014.assessment: Communal massacres. Anti-Balaka and ex-Séléka atrocities. Mass displacement. No civil liberties across most of country. FH CL=7.

2015.assessment: Transitional period. Violence continuing. Elections preparation. No civil liberties — still extreme violations. FH CL=7.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elections — FH improved to CL=6. Some civil liberties restored in Bangui. Press freer. Opposition parties legal. Outside Bangui: armed groups rule by force. Severe restrictions overall.

2017.assessment: Severe violence 2017. Armed groups atrocities across country. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group entered — documented abuses against civilians. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions — Wagner adding new abuse dimension.

sources.citation: Freedom House: CAR 2018 — CL 6; Wagner abuses

2019.assessment: APRC peace. Wagner operations. FH CL=6. Severe restrictions.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive December 2020. Massive civilian rights violations. FH downgraded to CL=7 due to Wagner and CPC atrocities. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House: CAR 2020 — CL 7; CPC/Wagner atrocities

2021.assessment: Wagner atrocities extensively documented by UN. Extrajudicial killings, torture. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House: CAR 2021 — CL 7; UN Wagner abuses

2022.assessment: Wagner/Africa Corps abuses continuing. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2023.assessment: Africa Corps (post-Wagner mutiny). Abuses continuing. Constitutional referendum eroded freedoms. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2024.assessment: Africa Corps. Extended Touadéra term. Ongoing abuses. FH CL=7. No civil liberties.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Africa Corps; Touadéra extended powers; ongoing abuses. FH CL=7 projected. No civil liberties.

2000.assessment: Patassé faced chronic coup threats. Bozizé (Army Chief) dismissed in late 2001 after tensions. MLPC party factionalism. Militias loyal to various patrons competed. Active power struggles within the elite.

2001.assessment: May 2001 coup attempt against Patassé by former president Kolingba. Bozizé dismissed as Army Chief. Patassé relied on Libyan forces and MLPC militias (Mobutu loyalists). Elite deeply fragmented along ethnic/regional lines. Active power struggles.

2002.assessment: Bozizé launched insurgency from Chad with Chad mercenaries. Patassé relied on MLC (Ugandan rebel group) fighters. Military split between Patassé loyalists and Bozizé rebels. Government essentially besieged. Complete fragmentation of armed/political elite.

2003.assessment: March 2003 Bozizé coup. Patassé in exile. Bozizé dissolved institutions. Created Conseil National de Transition. His coalition was narrow — Gbaya ethnic group, Chadian mercenaries, former Bozizé rebels. Patassé loyalists went underground. Complete fragmentation — coup produced new dominant faction but rivals unreconciled.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2004 — Bozizé coup

2004.assessment: Transitional period. Bozizé consolidated power in Bangui but rural rebellion growing. Dialogue national process attempted. Patassé loyalists formed MLPC opposition. Northern rebels (UFDR precursors) emerging. Active power struggles — Bozizé consolidating but facing serious opposition.

2005.assessment: Bozizé won elections March 2005. New constitution. MLPC and other parties participated but distrusted. Northern rebels UFDR/FPR active. Bozizé controlled Bangui elite but northern/eastern regions outside his coalition. Open factional conflict with armed opposition.

2006.assessment: UFDR captured Birao November 2006. French Operation Boali supported Bozizé. Government military divided — defections to rebels. Multiple armed groups proliferating. Bozizé's ruling coalition strained. Open factional conflict with armed groups now controlling significant territory.

2007.assessment: Birao peace deal signed. UFDR integrated partially. Other rebel groups (APRD, FDPC) still active. Bush War ongoing. Bozizé elite coalition nominally managed but underpinned by French military support. Open factional conflict continuing in periphery.

2008.assessment: Libreville peace process. Multiple rebel groups negotiating. Bozizé government inclusive dialogue attempted. CPSK/FDPC/APRD all present. Elite competition channeled into dialogue process. Significant competition managed at national level while local conflict continued.

2009.assessment: Comprehensive Peace Agreement signed June 2008 implemented partially. Rebel groups nominally integrated. Bozizé held government together. Continuing tensions — LRA invasion from Uganda increased instability. Open factional conflict with continued armed challenges.

2010.assessment: Bozizé in relatively stable governance period. Peace agreements nominally holding. Parliamentary elections 2011 approaching. Elite competition primarily through formal politics. Tensions with ex-rebel factions not fully integrated. Significant competition managed.

2011.assessment: Bozizé won elections January 2011 (disputed). Opposition parties contested results. Ex-rebel factions growing restless over failure to implement DDR. Elite dissatisfaction building but not openly armed. Open factional conflict at political level.

2012.assessment: Séléka coalition formed December 2012 by UFDR, CPJP, FDPC — all ex-rebel groups integrated but not demobilized. They united against Bozizé over DDR failures and resource exclusion. Bozizé's coalition collapsing as army morale collapsed. Complete fragmentation — ruling coalition imploding, Séléka sweeping toward Bangui.

2013.assessment: March 2013: Séléka coup. Bozizé fled. Djotodia installed. But Djotodia immediately lost control of Séléka fighters — ex-UFDR, CPJP, FDPC factions each pursuing own agendas. Anti-Balaka militias forming in response. Elite utterly fragmented into competing armed groups with no unified command. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2013 — Séléka fragmentation

2014.assessment: January 2014 Djotodia resigned under African Union pressure. Samba-Panza installed as transitional president. Ex-Séléka split into UPC (Ali Darassa), FPRC (Nourredine Adam), MPC. Anti-Balaka also fragmented. Transitional government with no real control. Active power struggles among multiple armed factions.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum on National Reconciliation July 2015. Armed groups signed ceasefire. New constitution passed December 2015. Transitional government nominally inclusive. But armed groups retained territorial control — forum was paper agreement. Active power struggles with nominal reconciliation process.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2015 — Bangui Forum

2016.assessment: Touadéra won elections February 2016. Legitimate elected government formed. MINUSCA supported. But armed groups continued controlling 60-80% of territory. Touadéra had formal authority without physical control. Active power struggles — legitimacy vs armed group reality.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2016 — Touadéra elected

2017.assessment: Worst violence since 2014. Armed groups attacking civilians and each other. 14 armed groups controlling territory. Touadéra government essentially confined to Bangui. Rome peace talks initiated. Open factional conflict with extreme fragmentation in hinterland.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group (Russia) entered December 2017-early 2018. FACA expanded with Russian training. Khartoum peace process with Sudan mediation. February 2019 APRC — 14 armed group signatories. Wagner providing parallel military structure alongside Touadéra government. Open factional conflict with Wagner as new external power broker.

2019.assessment: APRC signed February 2019 — 14 armed groups included in transitional government. Some armed group leaders given ministerial posts. Wagner deepening military integration. Formal power-sharing arrangement but armed groups retained weapons. Open factional conflict with formal inclusion masking tensions.

2020.assessment: December 2020 CPC (Coalition des Patriots pour le Changement) formed — 6 major armed groups (FPRC, UPC, MPC, anti-Balaka, UFDR, others) united against Touadéra election. CPC attacked Bangui. Touadéra called Wagner for emergency military support. Complete fragmentation — major portion of armed elite revolted.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2021 — CPC offensive

2021.assessment: Wagner/FACA defeated CPC offensive by mid-2021. CPC pushed back. Touadéra reelected January 2021. Repression of opposition political leaders and civil society. Wagner became dominant military force. Political elite increasingly compelled to submit to Touadéra-Wagner alliance. Active power struggles with Wagner suppressing opposition.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2021 — Wagner victory

2022.assessment: Wagner consolidated control. CPC continued but weakened. Touadéra proposed constitutional referendum to extend term limits. Political opposition suppressed. Wagner parallel governance replacing state institutions. Significant remaining factional competition managed through coercion. Open factional conflict with Wagner dominance.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2022 — Wagner consolidation

2023.assessment: Wagner mutiny in Russia June 2023 — CAR operations rebranded as Africa Corps under Russian Ministry of Defense. Seamless transition — same personnel. Constitutional referendum July 2023 passed — removed term limits for Touadéra. Opposition parties marginalized. Open factional conflict with formal consolidation.

sources.citation: ICG: Central African Republic 2023 — Africa Corps

2024.assessment: Touadéra entrenched with Africa Corps support. Armed groups weakened but not eliminated. CPC fragmented further. Local elections conducted 2023-2024. Some nominal power-sharing with some armed group representatives. Open factional conflict but Touadéra-Africa Corps dominant.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Touadéra government continues with Africa Corps support. Opposition fragmented. Armed group remnants in periphery. Government consolidating ahead of 2025 elections. Open factional conflict with Africa Corps-backed incumbent dominant.

2000.assessment: Patassé era. Parliament existed but dysfunctional. Courts operated but politicized. Armed forces loyal to patronage. Severely degraded institutions — functional only in name.

2001.assessment: Coup attempt damaged institutions. Patassé using Libyan forces to counter Bozizé indicates state security forces unreliable. Severely degraded.

2002.assessment: Patassé under siege with Bozizé insurgency in north. Government unable to project authority. Severely degraded — collapsed or nominal in north.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. Dissolved parliament and constitution. Transitional government. No institutions — no functioning institutions during transition.

2004.assessment: Transitional National Council serving as interim parliament. Constitutional process beginning. Some institution rebuilding. Collapsed or nominal — transitional bodies not fully functional.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected — new constitution and parliament. Formally democratic institutions established. But parliament weak, courts politicized, armed forces unreliable. Severely degraded — formally re-established but weakly functional.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2005 — Bozizé election; weak institutions

2006.assessment: Parliament functioning marginally. UFDR offensive showing government weakness. FACA unable to defend north. Severely degraded.

2007.assessment: Libreville peace. Some rebel integration into government (GURN — Government of National Unity). Minimal institutional function. Severely degraded.

2008.assessment: LRA added to institutional stress. FACA incapable of responding. Severely degraded.

2009.assessment: Inclusive Political Dialogue. Some institutional normalization. Parliament operational. Courts marginally functional in Bangui. Severely degraded.

2010.assessment: Elections preparation. Government institutions functional in Bangui but not elsewhere. Severely degraded.

2011.assessment: Bozizé re-elected in disputed elections. Parliament formed. Institutions formally operational but weak. Severely degraded.

2012.assessment: Séléka offensive from December 2012 rapidly overrunning northern government structures. Government losing institutional reach. Collapsed or nominal as Séléka advanced.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2012 — Séléka advance; institutions collapsing

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013 dissolved all institutions. Djotodia unable to control even Séléka itself. No parliament, no courts, no functional administration. No functioning institutions.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2013 — Séléka coup; institutions destroyed

2014.assessment: Samba-Panza transitional government. Transitional National Council established. MINUSCA deployed. Some rudimentary institutional function being restored in Bangui. No functioning institutions in most of country.

2015.assessment: New constitution adopted December 2015 via referendum. Bangui Forum legitimization. Election preparations. Transitional government with minimal institutional capacity. Collapsed or nominal — transitional institutions only.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elected February 2016. Parliament elected. Formal constitutional government restored. Courts beginning to function in Bangui. Armed forces still weak. Severely degraded — formal institutions restored but very weak capacity.

2017.assessment: Government institutions in Bangui operating formally. Violence across country meant reach extremely limited. Parliament operational. Courts barely functional. Severely degraded.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group arrived — operating parallel to and bypassing formal institutions. Khartoum peace process nominally through government. Parliament operating. Courts marginally. Wagner presence undermined formal civilian control. Severely degraded.

2019.assessment: APRC incorporated armed groups into peace framework. Some armed group members in government. Wagner operating. Severely degraded institutions.

2020.assessment: Elections held December 2020 despite CPC attack — significant institutional achievement. Election commission functional. Parliament newly elected. Severely degraded but improving with Wagner support.

2021.assessment: Touadéra re-elected. Government extending reach with Wagner. Parliament operating. Courts functional in Bangui. Severely degraded — formal institutions exist but weakly.

2022.assessment: Government/Wagner controlling most prefectures. Administration extending. Severely degraded but improving — Wagner bypasses normal state authority but government reach expanding.

2023.assessment: Africa Corps. Constitutional referendum extending Touadéra term. Formal institutions nominally functioning. Severely degraded.

2024.assessment: Constitutional referendum to extend Touadéra term and expand executive power. Parliament weakened. Africa Corps operating. Severely degraded — formal institutions increasingly authoritarian/Russia-dependent.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Touadéra with extended powers, Africa Corps, weakened institutions. Severely degraded.

2000.assessment: Patassé era. Multiple armed group activity, coup attempt planning. Relatively lower violence compared to later years but chronic instability. Hundreds of deaths annually from armed conflict. Low-level chronic violence.

2001.assessment: Bozizé coup attempt May 2001 — Bangui street fighting with hundreds of deaths. Libyan-backed suppression of Bozizé. Moderate violence — significant in Bangui but short-lived coup attempt.

sources.citation: ACLED: CAR 2001 — Bozizé coup attempt violence

2002.assessment: Bozizé insurgency from Chad with LRA and other forces. Fighting in northern CAR. Moderate casualties. Moderate political violence.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003. Bangui fighting during coup. Post-coup consolidation violence. Hundreds of deaths. Moderate violence — significant but contained.

2004.assessment: Post-coup consolidation. FDPC and UFDR rebel groups operating in north. Low-level violence in rural areas. Low-level chronic.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elections. UFDR in north, armed groups active. Low-level chronic violence in rural areas. Low-level chronic.

2006.assessment: UFDR captured Birao October 2006. French Operation Boali intervened. Significant fighting in northeast — hundreds of deaths. Moderate political violence with major battle for Birao.

2007.assessment: Libreville peace agreement. Reduced but continued violence. Rebel groups nominally ceasing fire. MICOPAX deployed. Low to moderate violence.

2008.assessment: LRA attacks in southeastern CAR added new violence. CPJP and other groups in north. LRA massacres of civilians. Moderate violence overall.

2009.assessment: LRA ongoing in southeast. Political dialogue. Continued moderate violence — LRA and northern rebels. Moderate.

2010.assessment: CPJP active in north. LRA southeastern CAR. Election preparations. Low to moderate violence. Low-level chronic.

2011.assessment: Bozizé re-elected. Northern CPJP conflict ongoing. Moderate political violence in north, LRA in southeast. Low to moderate overall.

2012.assessment: Séléka coalition offensive from December 2012. Rapid advance through northern/northeastern CAR. Fighting escalating. Moderate to high violence — Séléka offensive caused significant casualties.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Séléka atrocities against Christian communities. Anti-Balaka formation and retaliation. Bangui massacres December 2013 — thousands killed. Full-blown civil conflict. Severe political violence.

sources.citation: ACLED/HRW/ICG: CAR 2013 — Séléka coup; Bangui massacres

2014.assessment: Peak communal violence. Anti-Balaka vs ex-Séléka across country. MINUSCA unable to prevent massacres. Thousands of civilians killed. Ethnic/religious cleansing in many areas. Severe violence — worst year of crisis with catastrophic communal violence.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum peace process. Violence continued — armed groups attacking each other and civilians. MINUSCA protecting civilians. High violence though reduced from 2014 peak.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elections — some reduced violence for election period. Armed groups fighting outside Bangui. High political violence throughout country despite election.

2017.assessment: 2017 saw renewed escalation — ACLED recorded highest fatalities since 2014. UPC, FPRC, MPC fighting intensified. Civilian massacres in Bambari area and elsewhere. Severe violence.

sources.citation: ACLED: CAR 2017 — worst since 2014; Bambari massacres

2018.assessment: Wagner Group enters CAR. Khartoum peace process. Wagner engaged armed groups militarily — combined operations with FACA. Wagner-armed group fighting and Wagner atrocities against civilians. High violence continuing.

2019.assessment: APRC peace agreement February 2019. Wagner-FACA military operations expanding. Reduced but still high violence. Armed groups violations continuing. High political violence.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive December 2020 ahead of elections. Wagner-FACA counter-offensive. Major fighting around Bangui approaches and provincial towns. High violence — CPC offensive significant escalation.

2021.assessment: Wagner-FACA defeated CPC and retook many towns. Wagner atrocities against civilians extensively documented (UN Panel of Experts). CPC retreated to borders. High violence continuing including Wagner abuses.

sources.citation: ACLED/UN: CAR 2021 — Wagner defeats CPC; Wagner atrocities

2022.assessment: Wagner/Africa Corps consolidating. CPC at border areas. Reduced but continuing violence — Wagner abuses, border clashes. Moderate to high violence.

2023.assessment: Africa Corps continuing. Border conflicts with CPC remnants. Moderate political violence — reduced from peak years but Africa Corps/Wagner abuses continuing.

2024.assessment: Moderate political violence. Africa Corps and FACA operations. Some renewed armed group activity. Moderate violence.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Africa Corps continuing; armed groups at borders; moderate political violence.

2000.assessment: Patassé era. Several coup attempts (2001 Bozizé first attempt). Government of Patassé technically holds country but weak control outside Bangui. FACA (armed forces) fragmented. Large portions contested with rebel/militia presence.

2001.assessment: Bozizé first coup attempt May 2001 suppressed with Libyan help. Armed conflict in Bangui itself. Patassé survived but state authority weakened. Multiple competing armed factions. Large portions contested.

2002.assessment: Bozizé operating from Chad with rebel forces. Northern CAR contested with Bozizé forces. Government control limited to south and Bangui. Majority outside control — northern regions controlled by rebels/Bozizé.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup March 2003 succeeded — seized Bangui while Patassé in Niger. Bozizé installed as president. Transition period. Bozizé militias (Chadian mercenaries, CAR fighters) and FACA fragmented. Multiple competing authorities during transition.

2004.assessment: Bozizé consolidating power. Northern and northeastern CAR poorly controlled. FDPC and UFDR rebel groups operating. Large portions contested — significant rural territory under armed groups.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected president March 2005 in first democratic elections since 1993. Northern areas still contested with UFDR and other rebels. FACA weak. Large portions contested.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2005 — Bozizé election; northern rebels

2006.assessment: UFDR captured Birao in October 2006. Major rebel offensive in northeast. Government unable to retake without French intervention. Majority of northeastern CAR outside government control.

2007.assessment: Libreville peace agreement January 2007 with rebels. MICOPAX (CEMAC peacekeepers) deployed. Nominal peace in north but rebel groups still present. Large portions contested with fragile peace.

2008.assessment: Continuing instability despite peace agreements. LRA violence in southeast added new dimension. UFDR and CPJP continued operating in north. Large portions contested.

2009.assessment: Inclusive Political Dialogue peace process. Some rebel integration. Bozizé government nominally controlling more territory. Large portions contested — northeast and southeastern CAR still problematic.

2010.assessment: Relative stability in election run-up. CPJP active in north. LRA still in southeast. Government controlled Bangui and major towns. Large portions contested.

2011.assessment: Bozizé re-elected January 2011 in disputed elections. Government controlled Bangui and south. North and northeast nominally pacified but rebel groups still present. CPJP operating. Large portions contested.

2012.assessment: Séléka coalition formed December 2012 from multiple northern rebel groups. Launched rapid advance from northeast. By end of 2012, Séléka controlled large swaths of north/northeast. Multiple competing authorities — government losing territory rapidly.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2012 — Séléka coalition forms, advances

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 24, 2013 — Djotodia installed. Séléka controlled Bangui but fragmented immediately. Anti-Balaka militias formed in response. Second half of 2013: multiple competing militias. Government nominally in Bangui but no control elsewhere. Multiple competing authorities throughout.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2013 — Séléka coup; anti-Balaka emergence

2014.assessment: Djotodia resigned January 2014 under international pressure. Catherine Samba-Panza transitional president. MISCA (AU) then MINUSCA (UN) deployed. Anti-Balaka and ex-Séléka factions fighting throughout country. Most of CAR outside any government control — truly fragmented. Multiple competing authorities.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum peace process May 2015. Many armed groups signed accords. But implementation weak. Outside Bangui: dozens of armed groups control territorial enclaves. MINUSCA limited reach. Multiple competing authorities — state control only nominal in Bangui.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2015 — Bangui Forum; armed groups

2016.assessment: Touadéra elected February 2016 in internationally observed elections. Democratic legitimacy returned. But armed groups still controlled most of country outside Bangui. MINUSCA unable to pacify. Multiple competing authorities — government in Bangui only effectively.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2016 — Touadéra election; armed groups

2017.assessment: Violence escalated significantly in 2017. UPC, FPRC, MPC and other groups fighting each other and civilians. 80%+ of territory outside government control per MINUSCA. Multiple competing authorities — worst year since 2013-2014 crisis.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group (Russia) military advisers and fighters entered CAR to support Touadéra. Khartoum peace process. Wagner enabled government to expand control in some areas. Still 14 armed groups active. Multiple competing authorities — improving slightly with Wagner.

sources.citation: ICG/HRW: CAR 2018 — Wagner Group enters; Khartoum process

2019.assessment: Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation (APRD/APRC) February 2019 — all 14 armed groups signed. Wagner-backed FACA expanded control. Still most of country outside government. Large portions contested — improvement from multiple competing but still fragmented.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2019 — APRC peace; Wagner-FACA expansion

2020.assessment: CPC (Coalition des Patriotes pour le Changement) formed December 2020 by 6 armed groups including ex-Séléka to prevent elections. Launched offensive. Wagner and FACA defeated initial CPC advance. Wagner expanded to country's interior. Multiple competing authorities briefly then government/Wagner retaking.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2020 — CPC offensive; Wagner defeats CPC

2021.assessment: Touadéra re-elected January 2021. Wagner Group expanded significantly — estimated 1,000+ fighters. Wagner-FACA retook many provincial towns. CPC retreated to border areas. Government controlled most provincial capitals for first time in years. Large portions contested — improvement from post-2013 nadir.

sources.citation: ICG/HRW: CAR 2021 — Wagner expansion; provincial recapture

2022.assessment: Wagner and FACA extended government reach to most of country's prefectures. CPC armed groups pushed to borders with Cameroon, Chad, Sudan, DRC. Improved government territorial control — though at cost of Wagner abuses. Large portions contested still — borders and southeast.

sources.citation: ICG/UN: CAR 2022 — Wagner reaches prefectures; CPC at borders

2023.assessment: Wagner Group rebranded as Africa Corps after Wagner mutiny in Russia. Continued CAR operations. Government/Wagner controlling major population centers. Border areas still contested. Large portions contested — significant improvement from 2013-2020.

sources.citation: ICG: CAR 2023 — Africa Corps/Wagner; border contested

2024.assessment: Africa Corps continuing CAR operations. Touadéra preparing referendum to extend term. Some resurgent armed group activity. Government/Africa Corps controlling most major towns. Large portions contested — border areas and southeast.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Africa Corps/Wagner presence; Touadéra consolidating with Russian support. Armed groups still active in border areas. Large portions contested.

2000.assessment: No Open Budget Survey data. Patassé government — no published budget documents. Revenue collected primarily from customs and taxation with minimal transparency. Budget process largely internal to presidency. No information publicly available.

sources.citation: IBP: CAR budget transparency pre-2006 — no assessment

2001.assessment: No budget transparency. Coup attempt disrupted any budget process. Government accounts opaque. No information.

2002.assessment: No budget transparency. Bozizé insurgency — revenue flows disrupted. No information.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup. Institutions dissolved. No budget process. No information.

2004.assessment: Transitional government. Some budget process resumed with donor support. IMF program requiring budget framework. Scant budget information through IMF/donor-required documents.

2005.assessment: Bozizé elected. IMF PRGF program — budget documents required for IMF review. Public access minimal. Scant transparency through donor requirements.

2006.assessment: CAR appeared in OBS 2006 with very low score (~3-5). Some executive budget published for donor purposes. Legislative oversight minimal. Scant transparency.

2007.assessment: OBS ~3/100. Virtually no public budget documents. Executive budget submitted to National Assembly but not publicly accessible. Some IMF Article IV documents provide budget data externally. Scant transparency.

2008.assessment: OBS ~3/100. No meaningful public access to budget documents. Libreville peace. Scant transparency.

2009.assessment: OBS ~3/100. HIPC completion — IMF/WB requiring budget documents for review. Some IMF public documents available. Scant.

2010.assessment: OBS 3/100. Minimal budget documents published. Executive budget nominally submitted to legislature. Public information virtually nil. Scant.

2011.assessment: OBS ~6/100. Bozizé re-elected — some improvement in budget documentation. Executive budget published. No audit reports publicly available. Scant.

2012.assessment: OBS 6/100. Séléka forming December — no impact yet on budget process. Limited executive budget documents. Scant.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Budget process collapsed entirely. Government accounts destroyed. Revenue system non-functional. International sanctions. No budget information. No information.

sources.citation: IBP: CAR 2013 — no assessment due to coup

2014.assessment: Civil war. Transitional government (Samba-Panza) with no functional budget process. Donor aid directly disbursed bypassing government systems. No information.

2015.assessment: Bangui Forum. Some government fiscal capacity restored. Transitional budget process beginning. Very scant information — some donor-required documents.

2016.assessment: Touadéra elected. OBS ~5/100. Executive budget partially published. IMF program requiring documents. Scant.

2017.assessment: OBS ~5/100. Violence continuing. Budget process nominal. Some IMF documents. Scant.

2018.assessment: Wagner entering. Parallel fiscal flows from Wagner mining operations entirely outside budget. Official budget OBS ~5/100. Scant. Wagner revenues = no information separately.

2019.assessment: APRC. OBS ~5/100. Some donor-required transparency. Wagner revenues off-budget. Scant.

2020.assessment: CPC offensive. COVID. Budget process disrupted. OBS ~5/100. Scant.

2021.assessment: Wagner operations consolidated — significant mineral revenues off-budget. IMF required budget documents. OBS ~5/100. Scant for official budget; Wagner revenues completely opaque.

2022.assessment: OBS ~8/100. Some marginal improvement. Constitutional referendum. Africa Corps mineral revenues off-budget. Scant.

2023.assessment: OBS ~8/100. Constitutional referendum. Africa Corps. Some executive budget publication. Scant.

2024.assessment: Africa Corps. Elections approaching. Budget transparency minimal. Scant.

2025.assessment: March 2025: Budget transparency minimal. Africa Corps revenues opaque. Elections. Scant.

2000.assessment: No EITI membership. Diamond revenues under Patassé entirely opaque. Some Kimberley Process precursor discussions. Artisanal diamond mining informal. No disclosure, opaque.

2001.assessment: No EITI. Diamond and timber revenues opaque. No disclosure.

2002.assessment: No EITI. Bozizé insurgency disrupted extractive sector. No disclosure.

2003.assessment: Bozizé coup. Extractive revenues completely opaque. No disclosure.

2004.assessment: No EITI. Bozizé transitional government. Kimberley Process member (diamonds). KP provides some minimal disclosure framework. No membership, minimal disclosure — KP only.

2005.assessment: No EITI. Bozizé elected. KP member — some diamond disclosure. Gold and timber entirely opaque. No membership, minimal disclosure — KP only.

2006.assessment: No EITI. KP member. UFDR captured Birao — conflict diamonds feared in north. Some KP concerns. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2007.assessment: No EITI. KP member providing diamond disclosure framework. Bush War conflict diamonds minimal. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2008.assessment: No EITI. KP member. HIPC required some government finance transparency — indirectly covers extractives. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2009.assessment: No EITI. KP member. HIPC completion. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2010.assessment: No EITI. KP member. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2011.assessment: No EITI. KP member. Some IMF fiscal transparency improvements covering extractive revenues. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2012.assessment: No EITI. KP member. Séléka forming — conflict diamonds feared. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2013.assessment: Séléka coup March 2013. Kimberley Process suspended CAR in May 2013 — conflict diamonds. No EITI. All extractive revenue flows completely opaque under Séléka. No disclosure, opaque.

2014.assessment: KP suspension continuing. Civil war — extractive revenues funding armed groups. No EITI. No disclosure, opaque.

2015.assessment: KP partially reinstated 2015 for government-controlled areas only. No EITI. Bangui Forum. Most territory still conflict zones with armed group diamond extraction. No membership, minimal disclosure — KP partial reinstatement.

2016.assessment: KP partially in place. Touadéra elected — some extractive governance improvement aspired. No EITI. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2017.assessment: No EITI. KP partial. Violence continuing — armed group extractives opaque. No membership, minimal disclosure.

2018.assessment: Wagner Group (Lobaye Invest, Midas Resources) conducting gold and diamond extraction. Revenues flowing to Russia outside any CAR government reporting or KP framework. No EITI. No disclosure, opaque — Wagner extraction completely outside any framework.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner extractives CAR 2018

2019.assessment: Wagner extractives expanding. No EITI. KP partially in place for government areas but Wagner mines outside KP framework. No disclosure, opaque for majority of actual extractive activity.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner extractives 2019

2020.assessment: Wagner dominant extractives. CPC offensive failed to dislodge. Russia extracting gold and diamonds with no disclosure. No disclosure, opaque.

sources.citation: UN Panel of Experts: Wagner extractives 2020

2021.assessment: Wagner consolidated after defeating CPC. Mining operations deeply embedded. UN documented Wagner extractive violations. No EITI. No disclosure, opaque.
